3.synthesis: Paul opens with a torrent of praise: "Blessed be the God and Father of our Lord Jesus Christ, who has blessed us in Christ with every spiritual blessing in the heavenly places" (v.3). He then traces the sweep of salvation, all "in Christ": chosen "before the foundation of the world" to be holy (v.4), predestined "for adoption" (v.5), redeemed "through his blood" with "the forgiveness of our trespasses" (v.7), let in on "the mystery of his will" to "unite all things in him" (vv.9–10), and "sealed with the promised Holy Spirit, who is the guarantee of our inheritance" (vv.13–14). Each movement closes "to the praise of his glory." The whole purpose of God, from eternity to consummation, is summed up in Christ.
3.historical_context: "Blessed be God" (v.3) follows the Jewish berakah form of synagogue prayer, and in Greek vv.3–14 are a single 202-word sentence cataloguing God's acts before any petition. "Heavenly places" (v.3) appears five times in Ephesians, naming the unseen realm where Christ reigns and believers are blessed. "Adoption" (v.5) evokes Roman adoptio, a deliberate legal act conferring a new family, name, and inheritance — the adopted son was chosen, not born. "Redemption" (v.7) was the price paid to free a slave, and the "seal" of the Spirit (v.13) marked ownership and authenticity — replacing circumcision as the new covenant's mark, fulfilling "I will put my Spirit within you" (Ezekiel 36:27).
3.literary_structure: The passage is structured as a Trinitarian hymn of praise, moving through the work of the Father (election, vv.4–6), the Son (redemption, vv.7–12), and the Spirit (sealing, vv.13–14), each section punctuated by the refrain "to the praise of his glory" (v.6, v.12, v.14). The phrase "in Christ" (or "in him") tolls again and again, making him the sphere of every blessing. The verb "to unite all things" (anakephalaiōsasthai, v.10) — to "sum up under one head" — announces the letter's cosmic theme, and the eternity-to-consummation span sets up the prayer that follows.
3.theological_application: The believer's deepest identity is settled before time and secured into eternity: chosen, adopted, redeemed, forgiven, and sealed — all gift, all grace (vv.4–7). Such election is no cold doctrine but the ground of overflowing praise and unshakable assurance, for what God purposed before the world's foundation cannot be undone by present failure. The Spirit as a "guarantee" (v.14) — a down-payment on the full inheritance — gives present comfort: the indwelling Spirit is God's pledge that he will complete what he began. And the goal of it all, "the praise of his glory," reorders the Christian life toward worship.
3.christology: Christ is the all-encompassing sphere of salvation: election is "in him" (v.4), so to be chosen is to be chosen in the Elect One; adoption is through him, the eternal Son in whom believers become sons (v.5); redemption and forgiveness come "through his blood" (v.7). Above all, the "mystery" of God's will is to "unite all things" — heaven and earth — "in him" (v.10): Christ is not one element of God's plan but its very content and goal, the head under whom a fractured cosmos will be gathered and restored.

15.synthesis: Hearing of their faith and love, Paul does not cease to give thanks and to pray (vv.15–16) that God would give them "the Spirit of wisdom and of revelation in the knowledge of him," enlightening their hearts to know "the hope to which he has called you," "the riches of his glorious inheritance," and "the immeasurable greatness of his power toward us who believe" (vv.17–19). That power is the very might God exerted in raising Christ and seating him "at his right hand in the heavenly places, far above all rule and authority" (vv.20–21). God "put all things under his feet and gave him as head over all things to the church, which is his body, the fullness of him who fills all in all" (vv.22–23). Paul prays that they would grasp what they already possess in Christ.
15.historical_context: Paul's thanksgiving-and-prayer (vv.15–16) follows the standard Hellenistic letter form, here filled with theology. "The Spirit of wisdom and revelation" (v.17) draws on the Spirit who rests on the Davidic shoot (Isaiah 11:2), and "the knowledge of him" echoes the new-covenant promise "they shall all know me" (Jeremiah 31:34). Paul piles up four Greek power-words (v.19) in doxological style, evoking the Exodus "mighty hand." The right-hand enthronement (v.20) is the place of supreme honor in royal court protocol, fulfilling Psalms 110:1, and "all things under his feet" cites Psalms 8:6. The head-body image (vv.22–23) fuses Hellenistic body-politic and Jewish wisdom traditions.
15.literary_structure: This first of the letter's two great prayers (cf. Ephesians 3:14–21) turns the doxology of vv.3–14 into intercession, asking that the readers come to know experientially what is theirs. It is structured around three objects of knowledge — hope, inheritance, and power (vv.18–19) — the last of which opens into a sweeping description of Christ's resurrection and enthronement (vv.20–23). The chain of citations (Psalm 110, Psalm 8) builds to the climactic identification of the church as Christ's "body" and "fullness" (v.23), introducing the body-of-Christ theme central to the letter.
15.theological_application: Paul prays not for the readers' circumstances but for their illumination — that they would truly know the hope, inheritance, and power already given them (v.18) — a model for prayer that seeks spiritual sight above outward relief. The astonishing point is that "the immeasurable greatness of his power" at work in believers is the resurrection power that raised Christ (vv.19–20): the same omnipotence that conquered death is available to the weakest saint. And the church's exalted dignity — Christ's own body and fullness (v.23) — dignifies the ordinary congregation as the locus of the cosmic King's presence.
15.christology: The passage presents the highest exaltation: the crucified Jesus is raised and enthroned at God's right hand "far above all rule and authority," with every power and name beneath him in this age and the age to come (vv.20–21). He is the cosmic Lord under whose feet all things are placed (v.22), fulfilling the dominion Psalm 8 gave to humanity and the enthronement Psalm 110 gave to David's Lord. Yet this universal head is given "to the church" as its head (vv.22–23): the One who fills all in all is, by grace, the life and fullness of his people.
